specific_mechanism: CUSP-Hyst: Cusp-Hysteresis Basin-Stability Test. Designate two LLM training control variables a, b (e.g., RLHF reward scale and curriculum mixture weight). Run forward sweep training γ_1: (a₀,b₀)→(a₁,b₁); reverse γ_2: (a₁,b₁)→(a₀,b₀). At each (a,b), measure capability score s(a,b; γ). Hysteresis H(a,b) = |s(a,b;γ_1) - s(a,b;γ_2)|. Large H signals fold-bifurcation region (cusp); refuse deploy.
llm_application: CUSP-Hyst: pick two training control variables a, b. Train a small probe model along forward path γ_1 = {(a_0+(a_1-a_0)t/N, b_0+(b_1-b_0)t/N) : t∈0..N} and reverse γ_2 = reverse(γ_1). At each control point, evaluate task accuracy s. Compute H = |s_forward - s_reverse| as the hysteresis signal. If H > τ_H anywhere on the sweep, declare the checkpoint inside the cusp region; require additional training to escape before deployment.
novelty_claim: Catastrophe-LLM bridge exists (System Theory blog; npj AI 2026 phase-transition compression; arXiv:2502.20779 Triple Phase Transitions). But explicit CUSP-Hyst forward/reverse training sweep for basin-stability deploy gate is less common.
motivation_justification: Cusp catastrophe is the canonical 2D control-space fold-bifurcation model. LLM scaling shows similar phenomena (Wei 2022; npj AI 2026). The structural mapping is shared math, not exact mechanism transfer.
sub_mechanisms: M_1: Choice of two control variables (a, b)
M_2: Forward+reverse training sweep paths γ_1, γ_2
